title: The Macro Minute: Is the US labor market still deteriorating?

Happy Wednesday at 1342. It's your
skipper here Darius Dell to present our
macro minute for Wednesday, January 7th,
2026. As always, we'll start with the
executive summary from today's lit
morning note. So, let's dive right in.
Today's key macro question is, is the US
labor market still deteriorating? The
short answer is for the most part, yes,
albeit likely not as sharply as it had
been in recent quarters. Terms of
analytical nuance today, investors were
treated with a triplet of key macro data
that helps shape investor expectations
for the timing and trajectory of Fed
policy. Regarding the December ADP data,
the strong negative impulse in private
payroll supports our jobless recovery
thesis. The acceleration in median
annual pay for job changers did not
support our continued labor low labor
market turnover equals slowing wage
growth thesis. The declines in the
private sector hires rate and private
sector layoffs and discharges rate plus
the structured depressed levels of both
rates as well as the private sector
quits rate did support our continued low
labor market turnover equals slowing
wage growth thesis. The December ISM
services PMI data supports our resilient
US economy theme. The December ISM
services PMI data supported our view
that the resilient US economy is
emerging from the bottom of its U-shaped
slowdown. The December ISM services PMI
data did not support our jobless
recovery thesis. Leading soft data are
notoriously more volatile than lagging
hard employment data. So investors
should not overreact to a single survey
release. And finally, the December ISM
services PMI data also supported our
sticky inflation theme. Leading salt
data are notoriously hit or miss
regarding their implications for lagging
hard inflation data as well. As always,
members of our global investor community
can trust that our institutional-grade
risk management overlays, Kiss and Dr.
Mo, will help our portfolios navigate
these emergent risks better than
investors attempting to manage risk
without systematic rules or relying on
fundamental research alone.
Transitioning to our 42 micro dashboard.
As always, I'll wrap up with a question
from our community. This one's titled
valuations as predictors of long-term
returns broken or insufficient. Says,
"Hi, Skipper. I was a little surprised
to see you call the long-term stock
market returns as predicted by current
valuations chart the dumbest chart out
there. Just this week, uh, Neil How
himself was on Adam Tagert's channel,
singing the praises of Robert Sher and
John Husman, two of the main proponents
of such charts. Do you think their
perspective will prove wrong this time
around? I.e. stocks will perform well
over the next 10 years from current
valuations or do you think the labor uh
the predicted poor returns will
materialize but simply believe that we
should be considering trendtiming
momentum tools in the meantime so that
we don't ignore the path that markets
will take in reaching their predicted
returns i.e. ride the bubble while it
lasts and don't get out of the markets
ahead of time. Uh so uh great question,
phenomenal question. Let me start by
saying uh a couple things. One, uh I
have tremendous amount of respect for
Neil. Adam, obviously a good friend.
Both are are friends. Uh Bob Schiller,
former Yale professor and uh John
Husman. Four of the smartest people on
earth. Neil I work with for a long time
and and and obviously quite fond of. Um
you know, he's he's I'd say he's one of
the two smartest people I know. So, uh,
you know, you'd never want to be on the
wrong side of anything that they
conclude, uh, without doing a tremendous
amount of work. And so, uh, the reason I
the the reason I said it's the dumbest
chart of all time is because it
disrespects the path that markets take.
You don't get paid as an investor in
average 10-year forward or trailing
returns. There's nothing average about
the path that markets take. Markets
could go down 50% and they can go up
200% from being down 50%. It's called
volatility drag. You must understand and
respect the path that asset markets take
if you want to avoid volatility drag
which is the thing that makes you retire
not on time if at all and certainly not
comfortably if you manage to do so. Uh
and so uh the key takeaway in terms of
why I said it's the dumbest chart out
there is because the reality is those
those the chart is statistically
accurate. There's no denying the
statistical relationship between high
valuations and poor 10-year forward
average annual returns. But the reality
is is those poor 10-year forward average
annual returns doesn't mean that every
year the market's going to be up a small
amount over the next 10 years. What has
historically has happened from these
elevated valuation points is that the
market goes down a lot at some point to
skew the sample the mean of the 10ear
forward average annual returns lower.
But the reality is after the market
bottoms, it starts going up a lot and
very fast. Not enough to make the
10-year forward average annual return
exceedingly positive amount, but
certainly it does imply that at some
point after the crash or secular bare
market, which we think is a highly high
probability outcome uh from wherever
this market cycle peaks. After the
secular bare market, you're going to
have a secular bull market. And so
there's nothing average about that that
that that that that path. There's
nothing average about it. You could lose
30 to 50% of your money being long
stocks and then, you know, have it go
up, you know, 20, you know, 100, 200,
300% from that point and still be down
in volatility drag terms relative to
someone who chose to manage risk using
institutional grade risk management
overlays like Kiss and Dr. Mo. You
cannot disrespect the path that asset
markets take. There's nothing average
about any of this stuff. Average
analysis leads to average results and
average results leads to investors not
retiring on time if at all and certainly
not comfortably when they do. And so
finally, I'll wrap up and take you back
to the uh the end of the uh lead off
morning note today to show what is one
of my favorite charts of all time, which
is this chart we created to demonstrate
the impact of volatility drag, which is
why I think the chart is so stupid
because it doesn't it doesn't
incorporate the path that markets take.
But as we show here at the top of the of
this of the slide, the journey matters
more to your financial, mental, and
physical health than the destination.
Losing lots of money destroys your
mental health. Obviously, it destroys
your financial health. It destroys your
mental health. It destroys your physical
health via stress. It destroys your
mental health via the the impact that
ego elevated stress has on your life.
Imagine being married to somebody whose
portfolio is down 50% at age 50 or age
55 or age 60. Can you imagine that? Can
you imagine being the child of someone
whose portfolio is down 50% at age 50 or
55 or 60?
I'm sure a lot of you could resonate
because you remember from 2008, 2009. A
lot of you remember it from 2001, 2002.
But getting back into the chart, what
this chart shows is a very clear and
cogent way to describe what we on the
institutional investment side know as
volatility drag. And most people on
outside of institutional finance have no
clue what this is. But to me, this is
the most important thing in all
financial markets. It's the reason why
institutional investors manage risk is
because of volatility drag. So what the
bars show uh in this chart are the uh
two different investment strategies
based on two different investment
returns uh streams and from a sequence
standpoint but they both feature 50%
average annual returns. So the
investment strategy A which has an
initial investment of $100
goes up by 50% each year. So it rises by
50% each year. And so ultimately by
after year one, the portfolio is $150.
It's now 200. Let's use 100,000. Makes
sense. Now it's $150,000. Now it's
$225,000. And after year three, after
going up 50% each year is $338,000.
Investment strategy B also has a 50%
average return, but it gets cut in half
in year 1. And so now it has $50 to
grow, to compound from, and then it
grows by 100% in the subsequent two
years. So it doubles to $100 and it
doubles to $200.
Both investment strategies have what
that stupid chart I'm talking about, the
same average annual return, but they
both wind up with very, very different
amounts of money. And the very very
different amounts of money at the end of
the return sequence in fastes on
sequence is what we're talking about
when we're talking about you cannot use
charts like that because they disrespect
the sequence that markets take. You
know, from these elevated points in the
in the in crowded bullish positioning in
the positioning cycle, markets don't
just go, you know, we're going to
instead of going up 8 to 10 to 15% a
year, we're just going to only going to
go up 2% a year for the next 10 years.
That's not what happens. the market
crashes first and then it starts going
up very quickly after the crash. But
guess what? It's still you might still
wind up with not enough money or way
less money than someone who chose to
manage risk. And so that's the key
takeaway we're we're calling out here,
which is you cannot use those types of
charts as a as a as a as a as a risk
manage as as a as a guide to lead you to
your promised land of retiring on time
and company. It's not going to lead you
to the promised land of retiring on time
and comfortably because charts like that
disrespect the sequence of returns which
is way more important to your financial,
mental, and physical health than the
average return. So hopefully I I beat
that dead horse to a bloody pulp. Uh
we'll wrap it up there. Dar still here
presenting our macro minute for
Wednesday, January 7th, 2026. Uh best of
luck out there. Uh, if you guys uh get
this video, send it to Neil, send it to
John Husman, send it to our friend Adam
Tagard, and send it to anybody else you
want to send to because to me, I think
this is the most important thing you
need to learn as a non-institutional
investor. When you learn this, you
realize how much garbage you're being
marketed to by the general by people who
do what I do for a living regarding, you
know, average this, average that.
There's nothing average about the
sequence of returns.
And more importantly, the sequence of
returns is inherently unknowable. And so
this is why we do our jobs every day.
This is why we refresh our resp
management systems every day. It's
because we don't know the sequence of
returns.
Have a great day. Catch you back on
Friday. Cheers.
